coredata.dc:title: Design of Janus-like PMMA-PEG-FA grafted fluorescent carbon dots and their nanoassemblies for leakage-free tumor theranostic application
coredata.dc:creator: Pei, Mingliang
Jia, Xu
Liu, Peng
coredata.dc:description: Theranostic nanomedicines for imaging guided therapy, simultaneously monitoring the real-time response of therapy, have aroused great interest in cancer research. Here, the Janus-like poly(methyl methacrylate)-b-poly(ethylene glycol)-folic acid block-copolymer-grafted fluorescent carbon dots (CDs-PMMA-PEG-FA) were synthesized via surface-initiated atom transfer radical polymerization (SI-ATRP) of methyl methacrylate (MMA) in ethanol with the modified carbon dots (CDs-Br) as initiator, followed by the PEGylation via click chemistry. Interestingly, they could self-assemble into blank nanoassemblies with hydrodynamic diameter of about 150 nm and Doxorubicin (DOX)-loaded nanoassemblies of about 270 nm with high DOX–loading capacity of 67.2%. The DOX-loaded nanoassemblies could release >80% of DOX at pH 5.0 within 36 h, while that at pH 7.4 was only 2%, demonstrating the excellent on-off behavior on pH stimuli. A possible mechanism was proposed to explore the drug release pathway in vitro. Enhanced anti-cancer efficacy of the CDs-PMMA-PEG-FA/DOX nanoassemblies with folate receptor (FR)-mediated targeting function than the free DOX was revealed by the MTT assays, while the bare CDs-PMMA-PEG-FA nanoassemblies possessed good cytocompatibility. The cellular uptake result showed that the proposed fluorescent theranostic nanomedicines could be effectively internalized into HepG2 cells and release DOX into the cell nuclei.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Theranostic nanomedicine
Janus-like hybrids
Nanoassemblies
Fluorescent carbon dots
On-off release on pH stimuli
Zero premature leakage

xocs:meta.xocs:articleinfo: absattachment articleinfo articletitlenorm authfirstinitialnorm authfirstsurnamenorm cid cids contenttype copyright crossmark dateloaded dateloadedtxt datesearch datesort dateupdated dco docsubtype doctype doi eid ewtransactionid hubeid indexeddate issn issnnorm itemstage itemtransactionid itemweight openaccess openarchive pg pgfirst pglast pii piinorm pubdatestart pubdatetxt pubyr sortorder srctitle srctitlenorm srctype ssids alllist content subj subheadings suppl tomb volfirst volissue volumelist webpdf webpdfpagecount yearnav figure table e-component body mmlmath acknowledge affil appendices articletitle auth authfirstini authfull authkeywords authlast grantnumber grantsponsor grantsponsorid highlightsabst orcid primabst ref specialabst

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Self-assembly of CDs-PMMA-PEG-FA and DOX-loaded nanoassemblies
Synthesis and characterization of Janus-like CDs-PMMA-PEG-FA and nanoassemblies
Drug-loaded nanoassemblies and in vitro controlled release
In vitro cytotoxicity and FR-mediated cellular uptake

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: Program for New Century Excellent Talents in University
xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: Program for New Century Excellent Talents in University
Ministry of Education of the People's Republic of China

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This project was granted financial support from the Program for New Century Excellent Talents in University, Ministry of Education of China (Grant no. NCET-09-0441 ).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: FTIR spectra of CDs, CDs-Br, CDs-PMMA-Br and CDs-PMMA-PEG-FA hybrids.
TEM and DLS analysis of CDs (a, d), CDs-PMMA-PEG-FA nanoassemblies (b, e) and CDs-PMMA-PEG-FA/DOX nanoassemblies (c, f).

(a) UV–vis adsorption spectra of CDs, CDs-Br, CDs-PMMA-Br, CDs-PMMA-PEG-FA and PEG-FA in water solution. Inset images of CDs (upper) and CDs-PMMA-PEG-FA nanoassemblies (below) excited by daylight and 365 nm UV. PL emission spectra of (b) CDs and (c) CDs-PMMA-PEG-FA in water solution under different excitation wavelengths and normalized PL intensity (insert).

In vitro DOX release profiles of the DOX-loaded nanoassemblies at pH 5.0 and pH 7.4 (a) and the “on-off” released DOX from the CDs-PMMA-PEG-FA/DOX nanoassemblies (Inserts: digital images of the release results of the CDs-PMMA-PEG-FA/DOX nanoassemblies at pH 5.0 and pH 7.4 after 40 h) (b).

In vitro cell viability of HepG2 cells incubated with different concentrations of free DOX, CDs-PMMA-PEG-FA nanoassemblies, and CDs-PMMA-PEG-FA/DOX nanoassemblies with or without FA for 24 h. Each data point represents the mean ± standard deviation (n = 3).

Fluorescence microscope images of HepG2 cells stained by Hoechst and incubated with CDs-PMMA-PEG-FA @DOX nanoassemblies for 24 h. Each image from left to right field, represents cell nuclei in bright flied, stained by the Hoechst, DOX fluorescence, and the merged image, respectively.
Synthetic route of the Janus-like CDs-PMMA-PEG-FA hybrids.
Self-assembly of the Janus-like CDs-PMMA-PEG-FA hybrids and the pH-triggered DOX release from the resultant nanoassemblies.
Fitted DOX release parameters (k and n in Higuchi and Korsmeyer-Peppas models and correlation coefficient (R2)) from the CDs-PMMA/DOX and CDs-PMMA-PEG-FA/DOX nanoassemblies.

head.ce:title: Design of Janus-like PMMA-PEG-FA grafted fluorescent carbon dots and their nanoassemblies for leakage-free tumor theranostic application

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: State Key Laboratory of Applied Organic Chemistry, College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Lanzhou University, Lanzhou 730000, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: State Key Laboratory of Applied Organic Chemistry
College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Theranostic nanomedicines for imaging guided therapy, simultaneously monitoring the real-time response of therapy, have aroused great interest in cancer research. Here, the Janus-like poly(methyl methacrylate)-b-poly(ethylene glycol)-folic acid block-copolymer-grafted fluorescent carbon dots (CDs-PMMA-PEG-FA) were synthesized via surface-initiated atom transfer radical polymerization (SI-ATRP) of methyl methacrylate (MMA) in ethanol with the modified carbon dots (CDs-Br) as initiator, followed by the PEGylation via click chemistry. Interestingly, they could self-assemble into blank nanoassemblies with hydrodynamic diameter of about 150 nm and Doxorubicin (DOX)-loaded nanoassemblies of about 270 nm with high DOX–loading capacity of 67.2%. The DOX-loaded nanoassemblies could release >80% of DOX at pH 5.0 within 36 h, while that at pH 7.4 was only 2%, demonstrating the excellent on-off behavior on pH stimuli. A possible mechanism was proposed to explore the drug release pathway in vitro. Enhanced anti-cancer efficacy of the CDs-PMMA-PEG-FA/DOX nanoassemblies with folate receptor (FR)-mediated targeting function than the free DOX was revealed by the MTT assays, while the bare CDs-PMMA-PEG-FA nanoassemblies possessed good cytocompatibility. The cellular uptake result showed that the proposed fluorescent theranostic nanomedicines could be effectively internalized into HepG2 cells and release DOX into the cell nuclei.

ce:section.ce:para: As one of the main adjunctive therapy methods for tumor, chemotherapy has been widely used in clinical. The chemotherapeutics usually induce dermal side effects, due to their cytotoxicity on not only the tumor cells but also normal cells [1]. In the last decades, various stimuli-responsive drug delivery systems (DDSs) have been developed for the on-demand site-specific release of anti-tumor drugs, triggered by tumor microenvironment and intracellular signals such as pH, reductant level or enzymes [2], or the external stimuli (light, electric or magnetic field) [3]. Additionally, the safety and efficiency of clinically-relevant anti-tumor agents could be improved by incorporating the targeting functions (such as folic acid with high affinity to the membrane-bound folate receptor (FR) overexpressed on a wide range of human cancers [4]), which produce major enhancements in cancer cell specificity and selectivity over their non-targeted formulation counterparts. However, so far, some intractable problems should be faced, for example, leakage anticancer drugs before reaching the tumor site. For most of the reported DDSs, ~20% of anticancer drug would be released during the blood circulation, causing severe side-effects to the tumor patients. So it is still a challenge to explore smart DDSs with very low premature leakage even zero release in physiological media, by delivering chemotherapeutics more effectively and more selectively to the pathological site and/or by guiding them away from potentially endangered healthy tissues [5].

Most recently, research of theranostic nanomedicines for imaging guided therapy, which can simultaneously monitor the real-time response of therapy, has aroused great interest in cancer research, by introducing contrast agents into the DDSs [6]. Such theranostic formulations could be used for the tumor imaging diagnosis via X-ray computed tomography (CT) [7], magnetic resonance (MR) [8], or fluorescence imaging [9], accompanied with the on-demand site-specific release of anti-tumor drugs.

Carbon dots (CDs), a new class of fluorescent carbon nanomaterials, possess the attractive properties of high stability, lower cytotoxicity and remarkably biocompatibility, simple synthetic routes, and plentiful surface groups for surface functionalization as well as comparable optical properties [10]. They have been extensively investigated especially due to their strong and tunable fluorescence emission properties, enabling their applications in biomedicine [11]. Limited by their small size of few nanometers, the single CQD-based theranostic agents could not be clinically applied due to the clearance through the kidneys [12], although they had been intensely reported recently [13–19].

However, they could be used to design the CQD-based theranostic agents with suitable size favoring the EPR effect. For examples, Wu et al. designed a multi-functionalized, integrated theranostic nanoagent based on folate-conjugated reducible polyethylenimine passivated carbon dots, which emitted visible blue photoluminescence under 360 nm excitation and encapsulate multiple siRNAs for intracellular release [20]. Jia et al. fabricated multi-functionalized hyaluronic acid nanogels crosslinked with carbon dots following the Doxorubicin (DOX)-loading for dual receptor-mediated targeting tumor theranostics application [21]. Wang and coworkers designed redox-responsive controlled drug delivery and in vivo bioimaging by grafting fluorescent carbon dots to the surface of mesoporous silica nanoparticles (MSNs) via disulfide bonds [22].

Janus particles combination of two distinct sides with differences in chemical nature and/or polarity on each face, have been demonstrated as promising candidates for drug delivery applications owing to their dual structure [23]. In the present work, the Janus-like poly(methyl methacrylate)-b-poly(ethylene glycol)-folic acid block-copolymer-grafted carbon dots (CDs-PMMA-PEG-FA) were synthesized via surface-initiated atom transfer radical polymerization (SI-ATRP) technique with modified CDs as initiator and click chemistry (Scheme 1 ). Here, CDs were used as the cores of the Janus-like hybrids for the real-time and noninvasive location tracking to cancer cells. Poly(methyl methacrylate) (PMMA), which has been broadly utilized in biomedical domain as a nontoxic material owing to their superiority in excellent biocompatibility and biodegradability as drug delivery systems [24], was used as the hydrophobic segments for self-assembling. Additionally, the functionalized CDs were PEGylated to improve the biocompatibility and prolong the circulating time in vivo [25], modified with FA groups in the ends of the surface block polymer brushes to enhance the drug distribution in the tumor and arthritis, to achieve targeted diagnosis and the purpose of treatment though active targeting method [4].

Owing to the SI-ATRP precipitation polymerization in ethanol, the Janus-like CDs-PMMA-PEG-FA hybrids were obtained. Interestingly, they could self-assemble into blank nanoassemblies with hydrodynamic diameter of about 150 nm and DOX-loaded nanoassemblies of about 270 nm with high DOX–loading capacity of 67.2%, such size is favorable to their accumulation in the tumor site [26], while the single carbon dot cannot be used as DDS because such small size can be eliminated through the kidneys [27]. At the same time, to further explore the anticancer drug release pathway in vitro, a possible mechanism was proposed (Scheme 2 ).

Citric acid anhydrous (99.5%) was bought from Sinopharm Group Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. Poly(ethylene glycol) diamine (H2N-PEG2000-NH2, Mn = 2000, 99%) was provided by Beijing Kaizheng Biotech Development Co., Ltd. Folic acid (FA, 97%) was obtained from Tianjin Guangfu Technology. N-hydroxysuccinimide (NHS, 98%) and low molecular weight 1-(3-dimethylaminoprproyl)-3-ethylcarbodiinide hydrochloride (EDC·HCl, 99%) were obtained from Aladdin Chem. Co., Ltd. and J& K Chemical Co., Ltd. Propiolic acid (98%) was purchased from Aladdin Chem. Co., Ltd. Doxorubicin hydrochloride (DOX, 99.4%) was provided from Beijing Huafang United Technology Co., Ltd. All other reagents were analytical pure reagent (A.R.) and used directly. Deionized water was used throughout the experiments.

The amino-riched fluorescent CDs were synthesized by microwave-assisted pyrolysis of citric acid and ethylenediamine, as reported previously [21, 28]. Citric acid (2 g, 10.41 mmol) was dissolved into 20 mL of water. Then ethylenediamine (0.9358 g, 15.57 mmol) was dropwise added with the vigorous stirring until a clear and transparent solution was obtained. Afterward, the solution was placed in a domestic microwave oven (700 W) and sustained for 4 min. After cooling to room temperature, the black-brown CDs product was obtained by dialysis (MWCO of 1000) against deionized water for 3 days and lyophilization.

CDs-Br was synthesized by amidation reaction as reported previously [29]. 2-Bromoisobutyric acid (1.0012 g, 5.9 mmol, BiBA), dicyclohexylcarbodiimide (1.2235 g, 5.9 mmol, DCC) and N-hydroxysuccinimide (1.0012 g, 8.7 mmol, NHS) were dissolved into 10 mL of anhydrous DMSO with magnetic stirring under at 30 °C for 8 h, to allow for sufficient carboxyl group activations. Then the solution of CDs (0.2853 g) in 5 mL of anhydrous DMSO with 3–4 drops of anhydrous trimethylamine was added and the amidation was conducted for 48 h at 30 °C. The resultant black-brown CDs-Br was obtained by dialysis (MWCO of 3500) against deionized water for 3 days and lyophilization.

CDs-PMMA-Br was prepared via SI-ATRP with CDs-Br as initiator. 11.16 mg (0.11 mmol) of CuBr was dissolved into 8 mL of anhydrous ethanol in a dry Schlenk tube. After the mixture was degassed by three freeze-pump-thaw cycles under N2 atmosphere, 43.86 mg of CDs-Br, 10.44 μL (0.05 mmol) of N,N,N′,N″,N″-pentamethyldiethylenetriamine (PMDETA), 420 μL (4 mmol) MMA, and 5.844 mg (0.01 mmol) of NaCl dissolved in 10 mL of deionized water were added, followed by three freeze-pump-thaw cycles under N2 atmosphere. The polymerization was conducted with stirring for 8 h at 80 °C. The functionalized CDs were obtained by dialysis (MWCO of 3500) against deionized water for 3 days and lyophilization.

The FA-PEG-NH2 was synthesized via the DCC-activated amidation of one of the terminated amino group of NH2-PEG2000-NH2 with FA in DMSO as reported previously [21]. Then PC-PEG-FA was synthesized by amidation of FA-PEG2000-NH2 with propiolic acid, as following: After FA-PEG2000-NH2 (0.1 g, 0.04 mmol) was adequately dissolved into 10 mL of DMF, 2.8 mg (0.04 mmol) of propiolic acid was added dropwise and the mixture was stirred at room temperature for 12 h. Finally, the yellow powder was obtained by dialysis (MWCO of 3500) against deionized water for 3 days and lyophilization.

CDs-PMMA-PEG-FA were synthesized via click chemistry between CDs-PMMA-N3 and PC-PEG-FA. 83.86 mg of CDs-PMMA-Br and 16.25 mg of NaN3 (0.25 mmol) were dissolved into 20 mL of DMF with stirring at room temperature for 12 h. The mixture solution was dialyzed (MWCO of 3500) against deionized water to remove residual sodium salts. After the lyophilization, a yellow-brown CDs-PMMA-N3 was obtained.

0.15 g PC-PEG-FA, 43.86 mg of CDs-PMMA-N3, 10 μL (0.05 mmol) PMDETA, 0.016 mg of CuBr (0.05 mmol) and anhydrous DMF (6 mL) were added into a clean and dry Schlenk tube and the mixture was degassed with three freeze-pump-thaw cycles. The reaction was performed with stirring at 80 °C under N2 environment for 8 h. The CDs-PMMA-PEG-FA was obtained by dialysis (MWCO of 3500) against deionized water for 3 days and lyophilization.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Self-assembly of CDs-PMMA-PEG-FA and DOX-loaded nanoassemblies
ce:section.ce:para: The DOX-loaded CDs-PMMA-PEG-FA nanoassemblies (CDs-PMMA-PEG-FA/DOX) were prepared via a membrane dialysis method as reported previously [30]. Typically, 100 mg of CDs-PMMA-PEG-FA and 50 mg of DOX were dissolved into 8 mL of DMSO with stirring at room temperature for 0.5 h in the dark. After the mixture was added dropwise to 60 mL distilled water under magnetic stirring for 24 h in the dark, the DOX-loaded nanoassemblies were collected via dialysis (MWCO of 14,000) with distilled water for 4 days and lyophilization. The drug loading capacity (DLC, mass ratio between the loaded DOX and CDs-PMMA-PEG-FA) was calculated by determining the DOX concentration with a UV–vis spectrophotometer (TU-1902) at 480 nm, after the DOX-loaded nanoassemblies were dissolved in DMSO with stirring for 24 h.
The blank nanoassemblies were also prepared via the similar procedure without DOX for comparison.

The CDs-PMMA-PEG-FA/DOX nanoassemblies (10 mg) were dispersed into 10 mL of phosphate buffer solution (PBS) at pH 7.4 or acetic acid buffer solution (ABS) at pH 5.0 in the dialysis tube with MWCO of 14,000, respectively. The dispersion was incubated into 110 mL of buffer solution at 37 °C with shaking (rpm of 120). After a certain interval, 5 mL of dialysate was taken out for DOX measurement and the same volume of fresh buffer was added.

At the same time, to investigate the pH-responsive switch for DOX release, 5 mg of CDs-PMMA-PEG-FA/DOX nanoassemblies were dispersed into 10 mL of PBS (pH 7.4). The dispersion was transferred into a dialysis bag (MWCO of 14,000), and the dialysis bag was subsequently incubated in 110 mL of PBS solution with pH 7.4. At a predetermined time, 5 mL of the dialysate taken out for DOX measurement and the same volume of fresh buffer was added. After 5 h, the dialysate was adjusted into pH values toward 5.0 with 0.1 mol/L HCI solution and further incubated for 36 h. The DOX release behavior was tracked by the similar approach.

The cytotoxicity of the blank and DOX loaded CDs-PMMA-PEG-FA nanoassemblies against HepG2 cells were evaluated using the MTT assay. The HepG2 cells were seeded into the 96-well plate with the density 1 × 105 cells per well in 100 μL DMEM containing 10% FBS and cultured with different concentrations of the nanoassemblies at 37 °C in 5% CO2 atmosphere. After 48 h incubation, the culture medium was disposed and washed thrice with PBS (0.01 mol/L, pH 7.4). Then, 20 μL MTT liquor (5 mg/mL in PBS) was added to each well and 150 μL of DMSO was added to dissolve the crystal. Cell viability was measured with enzyme linked immunosorbent at a wavelength of 490 nm.

The cellular uptake of the CDs-PMMA-PEG-FA/DOX nanoassemblies was exhibited through fluorescence microscope (DM 4000B) using HepG2 cells after 24 h incubation to visualize the intercellular distribution of DOX. The cell nuclei were stained with by the Hoechst 33,258. The location of cellular fluorescence was validated with excitation wavelength of 480 nm (DOX) and 405 nm (Hoechst 33,258), respectively.
Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectroscopy was carried out on a Nicolet 360 FT-IR spectrometer of wavenumber (4000–400 cm−1) and samples were employed with the KBr pellet technique.
1H NMR spectra were characterized with a Nuclear Magnetic Resonance instrument (JEOL ECS 400 M) at 400 MHz with the solvent of dimethylsulfoxide-D6 or deuterated dichloromethane.
The steady-state emission spectra of CDs and CDs-PMMA-PEG-FA nanoassemblies were detected in their aqueous dispersions with a Hitachi F-7500 fluorescence spectrometer.

The morphology and diameter of the CDs and nanoassemblies were characterized using a JEM-1200EX transmission electron microscopy (TEM) with samples dispersed in water and deposited on each copper grid.

The particle size and polydispersity (PDI, size distribution) of the nanoassemblies were measured in aqueous media with dynamic scattered light (DLS, BI-200SM) at room temperature. Each sample was measured three times, the average value was calculated.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis and characterization of Janus-like CDs-PMMA-PEG-FA and nanoassemblies
ce:section.ce:para: The Janus-like CDs-PMMA-PEG-FA hybrids were synthesized via the ATRP and click chemistry approaches (Scheme 1). Firstly, highly fluorescent CDs were prepared via one-pot microwave assisted methods of using citric acid (CA), containing carboxyl groups to facilitate the dehydration and carbonization, as the carbon source and 1,2-ethylenediamine (EDA) as the surface passivation agent [21, 28]. The FT-IR spectrum of the CDs shows a broad peak concentrated mainly around 4000 cm−1 inferred that the existence of OH and NH2, as well as the absorbance at 1700 cm−1 of CO and 1435 cm−1 of CN (Fig. 1 ), indicating the existence of abundant functional groups on the surface of CDs. the FT-IR bands at 1659, 1562, and 1429 cm−1 correspond to the CN, CC, and CO stretching vibrations respectively [31], implying the formation of polyaromatic structures in the CDs cores. The results of XRD show a broad peak centered at 21.6° which suggest its disordered carbon atoms (Fig. S1) [32].

CDs-Br was prepared by amidation reaction mediated by the catalyst agent DDC and NHS in DMSO. Conjugation of BiBA on the CDs was confirmed by the appearance of the chemical shift at δ = 1.65 ppm in comparison the pristine CDs in the 1H NMR spectrum (Fig. S2). Then, CDs-PMMA-Br was synthesized by the ATRP of MMA with CDs-Br as ATRP initiator. Both the chemical shifts at δ = 0.82, δ = 1.85 and δ = 3.60 ppm in the 1H NMR spectrum (Fig. S2) and the characteristic absorbance at 1731 cm−1 of CO in ester group in the FT-IR spectrum (Fig. 1) revealed the successful synthesis. The CDs-PMMA-PEG-FA was prepared via click chemistry between PC-PEG-FA and CDs-PMMA-N3, revealed by the chemical shift at δ = 3.65 referred to the hydrogen of the liner PEG in the 1H NMR spectrum (Fig. S2) and the very strong peak at 1110.0 cm−1 of COC bond in the FT-IR analysis (Fig. 1).

The morphology and size of the functionalized CDs were characterized with TEM and DLS techniques. The bare CDs were 2.5 ± 0.5 nm from the TEM analysis (Fig. 2a). After grafting PMMA brushes via the SI-ATRP technique, which is a precipitation polymerization process, the Janus-like CDs-PMMA hybrids were obtained. Once an active propagating chain was generated from the CDs-Br in the polymerization, the produced PMMA propagating chain shrank due to its insolubility in ethanol, as a result, the adjacent initiating groups were shielded (Scheme 1). So the Janus-like nanoparticles were formed, with surface hydrophilic groups (carboxyl groups and the residual initiating groups) and the hydrophobic segments (PMMA brushes). The Janus-like nanoparticles could not be dissolved in water. After the click chemistry with PC-PEG-FA, both the Br atoms in the residual initiating groups and the active end groups of the grafted PMMA brushes were PEGylated. Due to the PEGylation, the CDs-PMMA-PEG-FA hybrids formed nanoassemblies in water with particle size of up to 80 nm (Fig. 2b). The hydrodynamic diameters of both the nanoassemblies from the DLS analysis (Fig. 2d and e) were much bigger than the particle size determined by TEM, revealing the surface layer of PEG segments.

The optical property of the CDs and their hybrids were measured by UV–vis absorption and fluorescence spectroscopy. Obviously, the as-prepared CDs and the initiator CDs-Br exhibited two notable peaks originated from the carbogenic core and surface defects: the one at 238 nm due to the π → π* transition of CC and the one at around 350 nm of the CN and CO transitions (both n → π⁎) respectively (Fig. 3a) [33]. The Janus-like CDs-PMMA-PEG-FA hybrids showed two absorption peaks, one is around 350 nm of CDs and the other at 280 nm is originated from the absorption of FA groups [34]. Additionally, the optimal excitation of the CDs at 350 nm in the aqueous solution and bright blue fluorescence at 365 nm could be observed (Fig. 3b and c).

The PL peaks shifted toward higher wavelengths, showing the excitation-wavelength-dependent feature of CDs [33]. With excitation at 340 nm, the emission peak for CDs occurs at 450 nm, indicating a Stokes shift of 110 nm. After modification, the CDs-PMMA-PEG-FA hybrid well retained the optical and PL properties of the original CDs (Fig. 3c). Such excellent PL property could be used for the fluorescence imaging guided therapy.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Drug-loaded nanoassemblies and in vitro controlled release
ce:section.ce:para: DOX was used as a model drug for the drug-loading and in vitro controlled release of the CDs-PMMA-PEG-FA nanoassemblies. The CDs-PMMA-PEG-FA/DOX nanoassemblies were obtained with high DOX-loading capacity of 67.20% ± 1.34%, after the DOX-loading via membrane dialysis method. The particle size (100 ± 30.0 nm) and hydrodynamic diameter (270 ± 40 nm) (Fig. 2c and f) were bigger than those of the CDs-PMMA-PEG-FA nanoassemblies (Fig. 2b and e), also revealing the successful DOX-loading. The DOX-loading capacity was much higher than that of the CDs-PMMA/DOX nanoassemblies (17.1% ± 0.34%) prepared with the similar condition. In such case, DOX should be mainly loaded via the electrostatic interaction with the surface carboxyl groups in the CDs-PMMA, along with a little amount via hydrophobic interaction with PMMA. As in the CDs-PMMA-PEG-FA/DOX nanoassemblies, the DOX was loaded via both the electrostatic interaction with the surface carboxyl groups in the CDs-PMMA and the hydrogen bond with the PEG-FA segments.

The in vitro controlled release profiles were evaluated under two different media, such as pH 5.0 acetic acid buffer solutions (ABS) mimicking the tumor microenvironment and pH 7.4 phosphate buffer solutions (PBS) simulating the normal physiological surroundings. The results demonstrated that the CDs-PMMA-PEG-FA/DOX nanoassemblies showed a highly pH value dependency in vitro release behavior (Fig. 4 ). After shaking the dialysis bag in the pH 7.4 PBS for 40 h, the solution was still colorless, while it turned brown in the pH 5.0 ABS owing to the DOX release. No burst release was found for all the release profiles. At pH 5.0, DOX was released rapidly with a accumulative release up to 83.2% within 36 h at 37 °C (Fig. 4a). By contrast, almost “zero” premature leakage was achieved at pH 7.4 conduction, the accumulative release rate only achieved about 2%. As for the CDs-PMMA/DOX nanoassemblies, the accumulative release rate was only 20% within 36 h at 37 °C.

It is well known that DOX is a spectroscopic and non-specific anticancer drug that inhibits the synthesis of DNA and RNA, then kills on tumor cells for various stages of cancer cells. But every coin has two sides, the healthy cells also suffer from the side effect of DOX, especially for the heart tissue cells. Hence, the on-off behavior for hydrophobic drug plays an important role in biomedical application to efficiently prevent the premature leakage during blood circulation. At pH 7.4 PBS, almost no DOX was released in the “turn off” state, it would be markedly helpful to reduce the toxic and side effects on the human body. Upon adjusting the medium to pH 5.0, most of the loaded DOX could be released, exhibiting a “turn on” state in the tumor microenvironment (Fig. 4b). So the CDs-PMMA-PEG-FA/DOX nanoassemblies are expected to be smart theranostic nanomedicine for tumor therapy, owing to the zero premature leakage during blood circulation and faster on-demand release in tumor site.

The DOX release mechanism from the CDs-PMMA-PEG-FA/DOX nanoassemblies was simulated with the Higuchi and Korsmeyer-Peppas models (Fig. S3). The Higuchi model expresses the diffusion-controlled release of drug from a carrier, as a square root of time dependent process based on Fickian diffusion, as following:
Higuchi equation :
M t
/
M ∞
=
kt
1 / 2

The Korsmeyer–Peppas model is a semi-empiric model, in which diffusion is the main drug release mechanism. It is used to understand the release mechanism only when M
t /M
∞ < 60%, according to the value of release exponent n, the release mechanism has different modes of diffusion as follows: a) n < 0.43 for Fickian diffusion, b) 0.43 < n < 0.85 for an anomalous diffusion (combination of diffusion and matrix swelling) and c) n > 0.85 for a non-Fickian diffusion.
Korsmeyer – Peppas equation :
M t
/
M ∞
=
kt n
where M
t and M
∞ are the amount of the drug released at time t and the initial amount of the drug in the DDS respectively, k is a release rate constant, n is the release exponent.

As shown in Table 1 , the best correlation coefficient (R2) values are obtained for the Higuchi model for all cases, demonstrating the DOX release is mainly controlled by diffusion [35]. As for the Korsmeyer–Peppas model, the DOX release from the CDs-PMMA-PEG-FA/DOX nanoassemblies at pH 7.4 was due to Fickian diffusion (n < 0.43) [36]. In addition, the release plots from the CDs-PMMA/DOX nanoassemblies at pH 5.0 demonstrated that it was diffusion-controlled release from the Higuchi theory (k < 1) and Super case II transport (n > 0.85) in the Korsmeyer–Peppas model. The different release mechanisms of the two DOX-loaded nanoassemblies should be resulted from their different structures.

To further explore the DOX loading and release mechanism from the CDs-PMMA-PEG-FA/DOX nanoassemblies, a possible model was proposed. In the self-assembling of the Janus-like nanoparticles of the CDs-PMMA-PEG-FA hybrids, the hydrophobic PMMA blocks in the hybrids shrank and agglomerated to form the physically crosslinked nanoassemblies with hydrophilic surfaces owing to the much higher content of the PEG chains, in which part of the hydrophobic FA groups might be enwrapped in the isolated PMMA phase [37]. As for the self-assembling in the presence of DOX, DOX was mainly adsorbed onto the surfaces of the CDs, covered with the hydrophobic FA groups (Scheme 2). So the average hydrodynamic diameter (270 ± 40 nm) of the CDs-PMMA-PEG-FA/DOX nanoassemblies was bigger than those of the bare CDs-PMMA-PEG-FA nanoassemblies (Fig. 2). After complete removal of the loaded DOX with the PEG chains on the surface of the DOX-loaded nanoassemblies during the dialysis procedure, no burst release was found for both systems, owing to the protective layer of the FA groups via strong interaction between with the adsorbed DOX molecules such as electrostatic interaction and hydrogen bond [38], the zero premature leakage was achieved at pH 7.4.

As in the simulated tumor intracellular micro-environment with high acidity (pH 5.0), the amino groups in FA were protonated to show better water-solubility. Thus, the PEG chains stretched out and the adsorbed DOX molecules were uncovered to release as water-soluble protonated DOX molecules. Therefore, a fast sustained DOX release was achieved from the CDs-PMMA-PEG-FA/DOX nanoassemblies. 80% of the loaded DOX could be released from the nanoassemblies at pH 5.0 for 36 h, while those loaded in the isolated PMMA phase could not be released. After the DOX release, their average hydrodynamic diameter decreased from 265 nm to 250 nm, which was much bigger than that of the bare CDs-PMMA-PEG-FA nanoassemblies (about 150 nm). It should be resulted from the stretched PEG chains in the CDs-PMMA-PEG-FA/DOX nanoassemblies after DOX release, in comparison with the fold-back PEG chains due to their FA end groups were locked in the isolated PMMA phase. Such deduction was supported by the contrast test of the CDs-PMMA/DOX nanoassemblies. Only 20% of the loaded DOX could be released from the CDs-PMMA/DOX nanoassemblies without FA-PEG brushes, in which most of the loaded DOX were covered with a hydrophobic PMMA layer.

ce:section.ce:section-title: In vitro cytotoxicity and FR-mediated cellular uptake
ce:section.ce:para: The in vitro cytotoxicity of the bare CDs-PMMA-PEG-FA nanoassemblies, CDs-PMMA-PEG-FA/DOX nanoassemblies and free DOX to HepG2 cells were evaluated by MTT assay. As shown in Fig. 5 , the bare CDs-PMMA-PEG-FA nanoassemblies showed no obvious toxicity (with viability > 90%) on the HepG2 cells at 0–20 μg/mL after incubation for 24 h, indicating their promising application for biomedical fields. Then the CDs-PMMA-PEG-FA/DOX nanoassemblies or free DOX with different concentrations was added in the incubation. Increasing the dosage, the cell viability decreased. The cell viability of the proposed theranostic nanomedicine was slightly higher than the free DOX in all dosage range. Considering the DOX-loading capacity of 67.20% in the CDs-PMMA-PEG-FA/DOX nanoassemblies, that means a drug content of 0.40 g DOX/g nanomedicine, as well as the sustained release (82% within 24 h), the CDs-PMMA-PEG-FA/DOX nanoassemblies possessed an enhanced anti-cancer efficacy than the free DOX. The half maximal inhibitory concentration (IC50) of the proposed theranostic nanomedicine for HepG2 cells was found to be about 20 μg/mL by the MTT assay in comparison to that of DOX (10 μg/mL).

To reveal of the FR-mediated targeting function of the CDs-PMMA-PEG-FA/DOX nanoassemblies, the cytotoxicity of the theranostic nanomedicine was also conducted with extra addition of 2.0 wt% FA in cell culture media, which interferes with the targeting role of the surface FA groups on the nanoassemblies. With the same DOX concentrations, the cell viabilities for the CDs-PMMA-PEG-FA/DOX + FA were significantly higher than the CDs-PMMA-PEG-FA/DOX without FA (Fig. 5). This result suggested that the CDs-PMMA-PEG-FA/DOX nanoassemblies were capable of the FR-mediated targeting-controlled delivery of DOX [39].

The cellular uptake of the CDs-PMMA-PEG-FA/DOX nanoassemblies was investigated by fluorescence microscope. 20 μg/mL of the nanoassemblies was added on 24-hole cell culture plate and the HepG2 cells were incubated for 48 h. Before imaging, the cell nuclei were stained with Hoechst 33,258, and the cells were fixed with 4% paraformaldehyde. It is obvious that the red fluorescence positions (released DOX) were completely overlapped with blue fluorescence (cell nuclei stained with Hoechst 33,258) in the merged image (Fig. 6 ), indicating that the loaded DOX was successfully released in the cell nuclei. The results demonstrated that the CDs-PMMA-PEG-FA/DOX nanoassemblies could be effectively internalized into HepG2 cells and triggered DOX release into the cell nuclei, by means of the FR-mediated targeting characteristics.

In summary, the Janus-like poly(methyl methacrylate)-b-poly(ethylene glycol)-folic acid block-copolymer-grafted carbon dots (CDs-PMMA-PEG-FA) were synthesized via surface-initiated atom transfer radical polymerization (SI-ATRP) of methyl methacrylate (MMA) in ethanol with modified fluorescent carbon dots (CDs-Br) as initiator, followed by the PEGylation via click chemistry. They could self-assemble into nanoassemblies with hydrodynamic diameter of about 150 nm and the Doxorubicin (DOX)-loaded nanoassemblies of about 270 nm with high DOX–loading capacity of 67.2% in the presence of DOX. The DOX-loaded nanoassemblies could release >80% of DOX at pH 5.0 within 36 h, while that at pH 7.4 was only 2%, demonstrating the excellent on-off release behavior on pH stimuli. The zero premature leakage in physiological media would eliminate efficiently the toxic and side effects of anti-cancer drugs. By comparing the DOX-loading and in vitro release behavior with the CDs-PMMA/DOX nanoassemblies, a possible drug-loading and pH-triggered release mechanism was proposed for the CDs-PMMA-PEG-FA/DOX nanoassemblies. Enhanced anti-cancer efficacy of the CDs-PMMA-PEG-FA/DOX nanoassemblies with DOX content of 40% than the free DOX was revealed by the MTT assays with IC50 of 20 μg/mL in comparison with that of 10 μg/mL of the free DOX, demonstrating the FR-mediated targeting function, while the bare CDs-PMMA-PEG-FA nanoassemblies possessed good cytocompatibility. The cellular uptake result showed that the proposed theranostic nanomedicines could be effectively internalized into HepG2 cells and triggered DOX release into the cell nuclei. Such features make the proposed fluorescent CDs-PMMA-PEG-FA/DOX nanoassemblies promising leakage-free theranostic nanomedicines for imaging guided therapy.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This project was granted financial support from the Program for New Century Excellent Talents in University, Ministry of Education of China (Grant no. NCET-09-0441).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.matdes.2018.06.007.
